topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

The screen glowed accusingly in Clara’s hand. A shaky breath escaped her lips as she reread the message she’d just dispatched. It was meant for her boss, a carefully worded update on the quarterly projections, but the recipient field had been a careless swipe, landing squarely on her estranged sister, Sarah. A cold knot tightened in Clara’s stomach, pulling her shoulders up toward her ears. She stared at the 'Sent' confirmation, the digital ink feeling like a physical stain on her conscience. She wanted to delete it, to rewind the milliseconds, but the option was gone. A slow, heavy sigh escaped her, and she slowly lowered the phone, the silence of her apartment amplifying the frantic drumming of her pulse.

[story 2]

Mark watched the notification pop up, a sickening lurch in his chest stealing the air from his lungs. The text, a rambling, overly familiar anecdote he’d meant for his new colleague, landed in the inbox of his grandmother. His jaw clenched so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. He rubbed his temples, the fluorescent office lights suddenly too harsh, illuminating the sheer stupidity of his oversight. Every muscle in his neck felt tense. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic warm against his palms, and just stared at the lukewarm liquid, the bitterness mirroring the sour taste rising in his mouth. He knew, with a certainty that chilled him to the bone, that this mistake would take time to unravel.

[story 3]

Liam felt a sudden, sharp wave of nausea wash over him as he saw the recipient name flash on his screen. It was supposed to be a private message to his romantic interest, a delicate confession he’d painstakingly crafted. Instead, it had been sent to his former professor, a man he hadn't spoken to in years. Liam’s hands began to tremble, the phone slipping slightly in his grip. He stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. A deep, shaky exhale escaped him, a sound devoid of any real strength. He felt utterly exposed, as if every secret he held was now laid bare on that digital page.
